ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 226934003 maps to beef chuck steak as a food substance; lean/raw qualifier not explicitly encoded in SNOMED CT hierarchy, hence 'closest_parent' match type.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw serving of lean beef chuck steak is approximately 120 g, equivalent to one palm-sized portion before cooking, yielding roughly 85–100 g cooked.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, chuck, arm pot roast, separable lean only, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 13327); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database for beef chuck lean raw
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database (beef chuck steak, raw, lean) with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used to confirm and supplement secondary nutrient values including saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, vitamins, and minerals. Values are consistent between both approved sources.

gi_source.title: N/A — Beef Chuck Steak, Raw, Lean contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g per 100 g). Glycemic index is not applicable for pure protein and fat foods. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. No GI value is meaningful for this food; gi_category set to not_applicable. The numeric value 0 is used as a sentinel to satisfy the template requirement for a numeric field while gi_category explicitly signals non-applicability.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (62 mg/100 g) with high potassium (360 mg/100 g) yielding a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles
hypertension.guidance: Lean beef chuck steak is suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared without added salt or high-sodium sauces; the naturally low sodium and high potassium content supports blood pressure management within a DASH-aligned dietary pattern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar; high protein (23 g/100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability without raising postprandial blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lean beef chuck steak has no glycaemic impact and is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; limit portions to 90–120 g cooked and pair with non-starchy vegetables to maintain a balanced macronutrient profile.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (3.4 g/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (1.3 g/100 g); however, cholesterol is 67 mg/100 g and trace naturally occurring ruminant trans fat is present at 0.1 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lean beef chuck steak may be included in a heart-healthy diet in moderate portions (≤90 g cooked, 2–3 times per week) due to its low saturated fat content, but individuals with established cardiovascular disease should monitor total red meat intake and avoid high-fat cooking methods or added saturated fats.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat contains arachidonic acid which may promote pro-inflammatory cytokine production relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; no anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, fibre) present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat including lean beef chuck to no more than 1–2 servings per week and consider substituting with anti-inflammatory protein sources such as oily fish or legumes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef chuck steak, raw and lean, is an uncooked cut of beef taken from the shoulder chuck primal of the animal. The chuck region sits between the neck and the rib, producing a moderately tough but flavourful muscle meat with characteristic deep red colour and visible marbling. In this lean preparation, all separable external and internal fat has been removed, leaving predominantly lean skeletal muscle. Raw lean beef chuck steak provides 124 kcal per 100 g, with an exceptionally high protein content of 23 g, low total fat of 3.4 g (of which approximately 1.3 g is saturated fat), and zero carbohydrates. It is a rich source of haem iron (1.8 mg/100 g), zinc (5.0 mg/100 g), vitamin B12 (2.1 mcg/100 g), and potassium (360 mg/100 g), making it nutritionally dense for muscle maintenance, immune function, and red blood cell production. The sodium content is low at 62 mg/100 g. Clinically, this cut is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its zero glycaemic impact, and for hypertensive individuals due to its favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio. Cardiovascular suitability is moderate given the presence of cholesterol and trace naturally occurring trans fat. For rheumatoid arthritis, red meat consumption should be limited. Chuck steak is commonly braised, slow-cooked, or grilled and is used globally in Western cuisines as a budget-friendly, protein-rich meat option.

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (data_source: hpb, confidence: 0.95) with secondary nutrient values confirmed against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for lean raw beef chuck. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2. The food identity is unambiguous and the nutrient profile is internally consistent.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero (naturally zero): raw lean beef muscle meat contains no digestible carbohydrates, confirmed by both HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is a pure animal muscle food with no plant-derived components. Glycemic index field is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value to satisfy template numeric-field requirements; gi_category is explicitly set to 'not_applicable' to signal that GI measurement is meaningless for a zero-carbohydrate food per Project Veritas v2 rules for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. Saturated fat estimated at 1.3 g/100 g, monounsaturated fat at 1.5 g/100 g, polyunsaturated fat at 0.2 g/100 g, and trans fat at 0.1 g/100 g (naturally occurring ruminant trans fat, not industrial) based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for lean raw beef chuck arm pot roast (NDB 13327), which is the closest approved-source equivalent. Vitamin and mineral secondary values (B-vitamins, vitamin A, C, D, E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as HPB database does not report these fields for this entry. Occasion tag 'breakfast' from input cultural data is noted as atypical for raw beef chuck steak; this tag likely reflects a database classification artefact rather than a common consumption occasion. Halal status is conditionally applicable depending on slaughter method; flagged as halal per input dietary_tags but this requires certification verification in practice. No ontology codes were invented; FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible matches to lean raw beef chuck. SNOMED CT code 226934003 is an approximate match with match_type 'closest_parent' and gap note documented.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 (satisfying the template requirement for a numeric field) with gi_category explicitly set to 'not_applicable' to signal non-applicability for this zero-carbohydrate food. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. gi_source documents the rationale fully. FoodEx2 code A0CKL (Beef, chuck, lean, raw) is a defensible exact match. SNOMED CT 226934003 is an approximate match with match_type 'closest_parent' and gap note documented. No codes were invented.
